questions.stem: The first-order autoregressive model AR(1) expresses $X_t$ in terms of which quantities?
options: its own previous value $X_{t-1}$ plus a white noise shock $\varepsilon_t$
a moving average of past shocks $\varepsilon_{t-1}, \varepsilon_{t-2}, \dots$
only the current white noise shock $\varepsilon_t$
questions.explanation: An AR(1) model is $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ (often with a constant), regressing the series on its own one-step lag plus a white noise innovation. A model built purely from past shocks is a moving average instead.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For the AR(1) model $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, the condition for (weak) stationarity is which of the following?
questions.explanation: An AR(1) is stationary if and only if $|\phi| < 1$. When $\phi = 1$ the process is a random walk (non-stationary), and $|\phi| > 1$ gives an explosive, non-stationary process.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For a stationary AR(1) process, the autocorrelation function takes which form?
questions.explanation: The ACF of a stationary AR(1) decays geometrically: $\rho(k) = \phi^k$ for $k \ge 0$. Since $|\phi| < 1$, this tends to zero as the lag grows.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: The general AR(p) model is written as which of the following?
questions.explanation: An AR(p) regresses $X_t$ on its own $p$ most recent past values: $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \dots + \phi_p X_{t-p} + \varepsilon_t$. A model in past shocks $\varepsilon$ would be a moving average.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For a stationary AR(1) with mean zero, the variance of the process is given by which expression?
questions.explanation: Taking variances of $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ gives $\gamma(0) = \phi^2 \gamma(0) + \sigma^2$, so $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2/(1 - \phi^2)$. This is finite precisely when $|\phi| < 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) process has $\phi = 0.7$ and white noise variance $\sigma^2 = 1$. What is the variance of the process, $\gamma(0)$?
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For an AR(1) with $\phi = 0.5$, what is the autocorrelation at lag 3, $\rho(3)$?
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: What does the partial autocorrelation function (PACF) of a pure AR(p) process look like?
options: it tails off gradually like the ACF
it cuts off (is zero) for lags greater than $p$
it is identically 1 at every lag
it grows linearly with the lag
questions.explanation: For an AR(p), the PACF cuts off after lag $p$: the partial autocorrelations are nonzero up to lag $p$ and essentially zero beyond it. This is the key feature used to identify the AR order.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: The Yule-Walker equations relate the AR parameters to which quantities?
options: the autocorrelations (or autocovariances) of the process
questions.explanation: The Yule-Walker equations express the autocorrelations in terms of the AR coefficients (and vice versa): for example $\rho(k) = \phi_1 \rho(k-1) + \dots + \phi_p \rho(k-p)$. Solving them yields parameter estimates from sample autocorrelations.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For an AR(1), the single Yule-Walker equation at lag 1 gives which relation?
questions.explanation: The lag-1 Yule-Walker equation for an AR(1) is $\rho(1) = \phi \rho(0) = \phi$, since $\rho(0) = 1$. This is the basis for the method-of-moments estimate $\hat{\phi} = \hat{\rho}(1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) series gives a sample lag-1 autocorrelation $\hat{\rho}(1) = 0.6$. Using the Yule-Walker (method of moments) estimator, what is $\hat{\phi}$?
questions.explanation: For an AR(1), $\hat{\phi} = \hat{\rho}(1) = 0.6$, because the lag-1 Yule-Walker equation is $\rho(1) = \phi$.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Which of the following AR(1) processes is NOT stationary?
questions.explanation: The process $X_t = X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ has $\phi = 1$, a random walk, which is non-stationary. The other three all have $|\phi| < 1$ and are stationary.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For an AR(2) model $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \varepsilon_t$, the Yule-Walker equation at lag 1 reads which way (using $\rho(0) = 1$ and $\rho(-1) = \rho(1)$)?
questions.explanation: Dividing the lag-1 autocovariance relation by $\gamma(0)$: $\rho(1) = \phi_1 \rho(0) + \phi_2 \rho(1) = \phi_1 + \phi_2 \rho(1)$. Together with the lag-2 equation this solves for $\phi_1, \phi_2$.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Looking at a correlogram and PACF, a series shows an ACF that decays gradually toward zero and a PACF with a single significant spike at lag 1 then nothing beyond. Which model is indicated?
questions.explanation: A tailing-off ACF together with a PACF that cuts off after lag 1 is the textbook signature of an AR(1). An MA(1) would instead show an ACF that cuts off after lag 1 and a PACF that tails off.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For a stationary AR(1) with $\phi = 0.8$, the autocorrelations $\rho(1), \rho(2), \rho(3)$ are which sequence?
questions.explanation: $\rho(k) = 0.8^k$ gives $\rho(1) = 0.8$, $\rho(2) = 0.64$, $\rho(3) = 0.512$, a geometric decay.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) process $X_t = c + \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $|\phi| < 1$ has what mean $\mu = E(X_t)$?
questions.explanation: Taking expectations of both sides: $\mu = c + \phi \mu$, so $\mu(1 - \phi) = c$ and $\mu = c/(1 - \phi)$.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) model is $X_t = 4 + 0.5 X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$. What is the long-run mean of the process?
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Why does the ACF of an AR(1) tail off rather than cut off?
options: because each value depends on all earlier values through the recursive structure, so correlation persists at every lag
because the model has a finite number of moving-average terms
because the white noise is autocorrelated
questions.explanation: Through the recursion $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, the current value depends on the entire past, so $\rho(k) = \phi^k$ never becomes exactly zero, only geometrically small. An MA model, built from finitely many shocks, instead has an ACF that cuts off.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: For an AR(1) with $\phi = -0.6$, what is $\rho(2)$?
questions.explanation: $\rho(2) = \phi^2 = (-0.6)^2 = 0.36$. Even powers of a negative $\phi$ are positive, so the alternating ACF is positive at even lags.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: In the operator notation $\phi(B) X_t = \varepsilon_t$, where $B$ is the backshift operator, an AR(1) corresponds to which polynomial $\phi(B)$?
questions.explanation: Since $B X_t = X_{t-1}$, the AR(1) $X_t - \phi X_{t-1} = \varepsilon_t$ becomes $(1 - \phi B) X_t = \varepsilon_t$, so $\phi(B) = 1 - \phi B$. Stationarity requires the root of $1 - \phi B = 0$, namely $B = 1/\phi$, to lie outside the unit circle, i.e. $|\phi| < 1$.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Using the operator form $(1 - \phi B) X_t = \varepsilon_t$, the stationarity condition can be stated as: the root of the characteristic equation $1 - \phi B = 0$ must satisfy what?
options: lie exactly on the unit circle, $|B| = 1$
questions.explanation: The root is $B = 1/\phi$, and stationarity requires $|B| > 1$ (root outside the unit circle), which is equivalent to $|\phi| < 1$. A root on the unit circle ($|\phi| = 1$) gives a unit-root, non-stationary process.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) process has $\phi = 0.4$. Compared with an AR(1) having $\phi = 0.9$, its autocorrelations decay how?
options: they do not decay at all
more slowly, persisting over more lags
more quickly, dying out after fewer lags
questions.explanation: A smaller $|\phi|$ means $\rho(k) = \phi^k$ shrinks faster. With $\phi = 0.4$, $\rho(2) = 0.16$ already, whereas with $\phi = 0.9$, $\rho(2) = 0.81$; the smaller coefficient produces a faster-decaying, shorter-memory process.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Consider the stationary AR(1) model $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ with $\phi = 0.6$ and white noise variance $\sigma^2 = 1$.
questions.solution: The autocorrelation at lag 1 is $\rho(1) =$ {{1}}. The autocorrelation at lag 2 is $\rho(2) =$ {{2}}. The variance of the process is $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2 / (1 - \phi^2) =$ {{3}} (to two decimals).
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: The stationarity of an AR(1) model $X_t = \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$ depends on the value of $\phi$.
questions.solution: The process is stationary when the absolute value of $\phi$ is less than {{1}}. When $\phi = 1$ the process becomes a {{2}}, which is non-stationary. For a stationary AR(1), the ACF $\rho(k) = \phi^k$ {{3}} toward zero as $k$ grows.
questions.explanation: Stationarity requires $|\phi| < 1$. At $\phi = 1$ the model is $X_t = X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$, a random walk. With $|\phi| < 1$, $\phi^k$ decays geometrically to zero.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) model with a constant is $X_t = c + \phi X_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t$. Suppose $c = 6$ and $\phi = 0.4$.
questions.solution: The long-run mean of the process is $\mu = c / (1 - \phi) =$ {{1}}. The lag-1 autocorrelation is $\rho(1) =$ {{2}}. Because $|\phi| < 1$, this AR(1) {{3}} stationary.
questions.explanation: $\mu = 6/(1 - 0.4) = 6/0.6 = 10$. For an AR(1), $\rho(1) = \phi = 0.4$. Since $|0.4| < 1$, the process is stationary.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Use the Yule-Walker relationship to estimate the parameter of an AR(1) from data. A series of returns gives a sample lag-1 autocorrelation of $\hat{\rho}(1) = 0.45$.
questions.solution: The method-of-moments (Yule-Walker) estimate of the AR(1) coefficient is $\hat{\phi} =$ {{1}}. The implied estimate of the lag-2 autocorrelation is $\hat{\rho}(2) = \hat{\phi}^2 =$ {{2}} (to four decimals). The Yule-Walker equations relate the AR coefficients to the {{3}} of the process.
questions.explanation: For an AR(1), $\hat{\phi} = \hat{\rho}(1) = 0.45$. Then $\hat{\rho}(2) = 0.45^2 = 0.2025$. The Yule-Walker equations link AR coefficients to the autocorrelations.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: Identification of an autoregressive model from its correlogram and partial correlogram.
questions.solution: For a pure AR(p) process, the ACF {{1}}, while the PACF cuts off after lag {{2}}. Therefore a PACF with significant spikes at lags 1 and 2 and nothing beyond, paired with a slowly decaying ACF, suggests an AR model of order {{3}}.
questions.explanation: AR(p): ACF tails off, PACF cuts off after lag $p$. Significant PACF spikes through lag 2 then zero indicate order $p = 2$, an AR(2).
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis

questions.stem: An AR(1) process has parameter $\phi = -0.5$ and white noise variance $\sigma^2 = 3$.
questions.solution: The lag-1 autocorrelation is $\rho(1) =$ {{1}}. The lag-2 autocorrelation is $\rho(2) =$ {{2}}. The variance of the process is $\gamma(0) = \sigma^2 / (1 - \phi^2) =$ {{3}}.
questions.source: in the style of Box and Jenkins, Time Series Analysis
